content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Reflecting on how AI is changing the way we look at results, experiences, and people through an incident involving two portraits
Recently, a Taiwanese online creator asked a street artist to draw a portrait of a family member while travelling overseas. Later, the creator used AI to generate an image in a similar style and placed the two works side by side, comparing which one looked more accurate and which one had a better result. The content received almost entirely negative reactions from Taiwanese internet users, and my first reaction was also one of discomfort.
I spent some time thinking about where this discomfort came from.
When two images are placed together, we can of course compare their resemblance, composition, and visual appeal. AI may indeed produce something faster, more polished, and even closer to what most people consider “beautiful.” But what really made people uncomfortable, I think, was that an experience originally shared between two people had been turned into a test of human ability against AI.
The street artist had created a commissioned work based on personal observation and individual style. The artist did not know that the work would later be compared with AI, nor did they agree to become the control group in a technology demonstration. Once the video was published, however, the artist’s observation, skills, and labour were reduced to material for a discussion about AI-generated art. The experience provided as part of the service was no longer something the customer appeared to value.
What feels uncomfortable is not that AI took part in the creative process. It is seeing AI act as something like a judge, and then using what AI does best to evaluate a real person.
But this leads to another, more difficult question: if AI can genuinely do something very well and quickly change the result according to our requests, shouldn’t we accept this kind of comparison?
In work that values efficiency, precision, and cost, it may be impossible to avoid situations in which humans are beaten by AI. If a technology can provide more consistent results in less time, we have no reason to pretend that it does not exist. Still, accepting that AI performs very well in certain areas is different from believing that every form of human expertise must be evaluated against AI.
Take portrait drawing as an example. You are in another country, enjoying the beautiful scenery, when a street artist begins drawing at your request. You wait for the work to be completed, watch how the artist observes you, and enjoy the peacefulness and beauty of that moment. In the end, you bring home a work marked by the artist’s personal touch. The entire experience sounds wonderful. AI may generate an image that looks more like a photograph or better matches common ideas of beauty, but the rest of that experience is still missing.
I have also started to wonder: as AI becomes increasingly capable of producing fast, polished results that are close to a standard answer, how much value is left in the act of objective evaluation?
In the past, objective evaluation helped us identify the best answer: which portrait looked the most accurate, which article was the most complete, or which design was the most refined. But now that AI can generate large numbers of results that meet these standards, is “getting a high score” still as important? By comparison, I may care more about why a work matters to me, what an interaction made me feel, and why an experience belonged only to me at that particular moment.
The coexistence of humans and AI that I imagine is not only about letting AI complete more work for us. It should also make each person’s experience more personal. AI can understand my preferences, extend my ideas, and help me find answers that better suit my needs, but it does not always need to decide who wins. Looking at the same portrait, one person may care about resemblance, another may appreciate the artist’s brushwork, while someone else may treasure the brief encounter with a stranger during a journey. These feelings do not need to be reduced to a single objective score.
Perhaps we still do not know what a mature form of coexistence will look like. AI is still learning how to understand human language, preferences, and needs. At the same time, humans are learning how to use it, how to face the comparisons it creates, and how to recognise values that are difficult to measure.
This incident reminded me that technology is advancing quickly, but the way we treat technology, expertise, and one another may not have caught up.
AI is learning how to become more like humans, while we are also learning how to continue treating people as people in the age of AI.
